mechanism_name: Light direction and stem bending angle
commonsense_rule: As one-sided light intensity increases -> the stem bending angle toward the light source increases (stronger phototropism)
factual_rule: As one-sided light intensity increases -> the stem bending angle toward the light source increases (stronger phototropism)
flipped_rule: As one-sided light intensity increases -> the stem bending angle away from the light source increases (stronger bending away from light)
key_visual_cue: The relationship between the number of right-side sunlight-ray arrows and the degree to which the plant stem bends to the right
visual_context: The sun symbol on the right indicates the light source position, and the number of yellow light-ray arrows indicates the intensity of one-sided illumination.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The sun on the right emits 1 yellow light-ray arrow (weak light), and the plant stem bends noticeably to the right, about 45 degrees.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The sun on the right emits 2 yellow light-ray arrows (medium light), and the plant stem bends about 20 degrees to the right.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The sun on the right emits 3 yellow light-ray arrows (strong light), and the plant stem tilts only slightly to the right, about 2 degrees, close to upright.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would happen to a young vine shoot under the same one-sided lighting setup if the light from the right became stronger?

choices.A: Its bending would determine how strong the light from the right becomes.
choices.B: It would bend more strongly toward the right-side light.
choices.C: It would show no reliable change.
choices.D: It would bend less toward the right and stay closer to upright.

prior_trap.reason: It matches ordinary phototropism intuition that stronger one-sided light should pull the shoot more strongly toward the light source.

partial_error.reason: It treats object substitution as if the visual rule no longer applies, even though the question says the new plant follows the same setup.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by making stem bending control the light intensity.

reasoning_chain: The panels show an unusual visual pattern: as the right-side light becomes stronger, the stem bends less to the right and becomes more upright. That induced rule should transfer to a different plant shoot under the same mechanism. Therefore, a stronger right-side light would make the vine shoot bend less toward the right.
required_induction: In this world, stronger one-sided light from the right leads to a smaller rightward bending angle of the stem.

question: According to the pattern in the image, which lighting condition should you choose if you want the stem to lean as far as possible toward the right-side light?

choices.A: Use any light level.
choices.B: Use a medium level of right-side light.
choices.C: Use the weakest one-sided light from the right.
choices.D: Use the strongest one-sided light from the right.

prior_trap.reason: It matches the normal expectation that stronger side light should create a stronger bend toward the source.

partial_error.reason: It invents a middle maximum that is not supported by the visual trend.

causal_confusion.reason: It incorrectly treats the plant's bending as the cause of the lighting condition.

reasoning_chain: The image shows that stronger right-side light is paired with less rightward bending, while weaker right-side light is paired with more rightward bending. To obtain the greatest lean toward the light source, you must choose the weakest one-sided light condition shown by the rule.
required_induction: In this world, stronger one-sided light from the right leads to a smaller rightward bending angle of the stem.

required_induction: In this world, increasing temperature makes the tree canopy less dense, not more dense.
